settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text:
## 1. Verdict
- Partial Pass

## 2. Scope and Verification Boundary
- Reviewed: delivery docs, backend/frontend architecture, auth/security middleware, core domain services (members, face lifecycle, content analytics, dashboards/reports, ops/backup), schema, and test suites.
- Executed: `backend` unit/integration tests (`npm test`) and `frontend` unit/component tests (`npm test`) successfully.
- Not executed: Docker-based runtime and Docker-based canonical test workflow (`docker compose up --build`, `./run_tests.sh`) per review constraints.
- Docker-based verification required but not executed: Yes (project declares Docker-first canonical runtime and e2e path in `README.md:76`, `README.md:167`, `run_tests.sh:55`).
- Remains unconfirmed: full containerized startup behavior, real MySQL runtime wiring in Compose, and Playwright e2e execution against live services.

## 3. Top Findings
- Severity: Medium  
  Conclusion: API rate limiting is not consistently applied across all auth/API entry points, so the prompt-level requirement is only partially met.  
  Brief rationale: The code enforces 60 req/min for sign-in and signed-session routes, but several API endpoints are reachable without any limiter path.  
  Evidence: `backend/src/middleware.ts:74` (sign-in limiter), `backend/src/middleware.ts:163` (signed-session limiter), `backend/src/app.ts:65` (`/api/auth` mounted without global limiter), `backend/src/routes/auth.ts:46` (`/bootstrap/status` no limiter), `backend/src/routes/auth.ts:141` (`/session` no limiter).  
  Impact: Increases unauthenticated/low-friction endpoint abuse surface (enumeration/polling/traffic amplification), and does not fully satisfy strict “rate limiting (60 requests per minute per IP)” wording.  
  Minimum actionable fix: Add a uniform per-IP limiter middleware at API router level (or at least all `/api/auth/*` endpoints) with explicit 60 req/min policy, and keep stricter endpoint-specific limits where needed.

- Severity: Medium  
  Conclusion: Canonical 0-to-1 runtime deliverability is not fully runtime-verified in this audit due Docker-only execution boundary.  
  Brief rationale: Documentation is clear and test suites exist, but the declared authoritative path is Docker-based and could not be executed under the imposed rules.  
  Evidence: `README.md:3`, `README.md:76`, `README.md:167`, `run_tests.sh:55`; local test execution succeeded for backend/frontend unit suites (108 backend tests, 47 frontend tests).  
  Impact: Final confidence in deployment readiness is reduced from full confirmation to partial confirmation.  
  Minimum actionable fix: Run `docker compose up --build` and `./run_tests.sh` in an allowed environment and attach logs/screenshots of health checks and e2e pass output.

## 4. Security Summary
- authentication: Pass
  - Evidence: Password complexity enforced (`backend/src/security.ts:91`), lockout after failed attempts (`backend/src/services/auth-service.ts:107`), inactivity expiry (`backend/src/services/auth-service.ts:221`), logout revocation (`backend/src/services/auth-service.ts:498`).
- route authorization: Partial Pass
  - Evidence: Signed-session enforcement and RBAC are present (`backend/src/middleware.ts:113`, `backend/src/middleware.ts:178`, `backend/src/app.ts:81`); partial due inconsistent global rate-limit coverage (finding above).
- object-level authorization: Pass
  - Evidence: Member-level checks in member and face services (`backend/src/services/member-service.ts:149`, `backend/src/services/face-service.ts:338`), inbox download scoped to requesting user (`backend/src/services/report-service.ts:570`).
- tenant / user isolation: Partial Pass
  - Evidence: Location and assignment scoping is implemented (`backend/src/services/content-service.ts:26`, `backend/src/services/member-service.ts:63`), but no explicit multi-tenant boundary model exists; tenant isolation beyond location scope is Cannot Confirm from current codebase.

## 5. Test Sufficiency Summary
- Test Overview
  - Unit tests exist: Yes (`backend/tests/*.test.ts`, `frontend/tests/*.spec.ts`).
  - API / integration tests exist: Yes (`backend/tests/routes.test.ts` using `supertest`; Playwright e2e specs in `tests/e2e/app.spec.ts`, `tests/e2e/bootstrap.spec.ts`).
  - Obvious test entry points: `backend/package.json:test`, `frontend/package.json:test`, root `npm run test:e2e`, `run_tests.sh`.
- Core Coverage
  - happy path: covered
    - Evidence: backend route/service tests passed; e2e scenarios exist for admin/coach/member workflows (`tests/e2e/app.spec.ts:69`, `tests/e2e/app.spec.ts:153`, `tests/e2e/app.spec.ts:209`).
  - key failure paths: covered
    - Evidence: backend tests include 401/403/signature/workstation checks (`backend/tests/routes.test.ts:308`, `backend/tests/routes.test.ts:331`), face-service failure paths (quality/liveness/dedup) executed in unit tests.
  - security-critical coverage: partial
    - Evidence: signed-request and RBAC paths tested (`backend/tests/routes.test.ts`), but canonical Docker e2e/security regression path in `run_tests.sh` was not executed here.
- Major Gaps
  - Missing executed evidence for full Dockerized end-to-end flow (including backup/restore and Playwright).
  - No executed proof in this audit that global API rate limiting uniformly applies at 60 req/min per IP.
  - No executed production-like verification for IP allowlist behavior under real network topology/proxy conditions.
- Final Test Verdict
  - Partial Pass

## 6. Engineering Quality Summary
- Project structure is credible for the requested scope: clear service/router separation and domain modules (`backend/src/routes`, `backend/src/services`, `frontend/src/components/composables/stores`).
- Validation, error handling, and observability are generally professional (Zod validation, centralized error middleware, DB-backed logs/alerts/access logs).
- Security-sensitive storage practices are materially implemented (AES-256-GCM service + key vault rotation metadata + masked phone display), with one notable policy consistency gap on API-wide throttling.
- Overall resembles a real product baseline rather than a toy demo, with meaningful tests and operational flows.

## 7. Next Actions
- 1) Enforce uniform 60 req/min per-IP throttling across all API entry points (especially `/api/auth/*` endpoints currently outside limiter paths).
- 2) Run Docker canonical verification: `docker compose up --build`, then `./run_tests.sh`; archive logs and health/e2e evidence.
- 3) Add one automated test asserting rate-limit behavior for currently unthrottled auth/session/status endpoints.
- 4) Add a short “non-Docker local dev verification boundary” note in `README.md` to separate what is guaranteed locally vs Docker-authoritative behavior.

You’re right to flag these. I’m implementing a uniform API throttling layer now (including `/api/auth/bootstrap/status` and `/api/auth/session`), adding a targeted regression test for it, and updating README verification-boundary wording. Then I’ll re-run backend/frontend tests.

payload.message: The Docker E2E failures exposed a side effect: the new limiter was being applied to all `/api` routes and stacking with signed-route limits, causing legitimate workflows to exceed the window. I’m narrowing the new uniform limiter to `/api/auth/*` only (which addresses the audit finding) and re-running Docker E2E.

content.text: The Docker E2E failures exposed a side effect: the new limiter was being applied to all `/api` routes and stacking with signed-route limits, causing legitimate workflows to exceed the window. I’m narrowing the new uniform limiter to `/api/auth/*` only (which addresses the audit finding) and re-running Docker E2E.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text:
1. Verdict
- Partial Pass
2. Scope and Verification Boundary
- Re-evaluated the previously reviewed evidence across README.md, backend auth/security middleware, route protections, core services, schema, and tests.
- Reused executed results: backend tests and frontend tests passed locally.
- Did not execute Docker runtime/e2e (docker compose, run_tests.sh) due the review boundary.
- Docker-based verification required but not executed: Yes (project is explicitly Docker-first in README.md:76, README.md:167, run_tests.sh:55).
- Unconfirmed: live container startup health, real Compose wiring, and Playwright execution against running services.
3. Top Findings
- Severity: High
  - Conclusion: Request-signing control is not consistently enforced across API surface.
  - Brief rationale: HMAC+nonce+timestamp is enforced only on requireSignedSession routes; key auth endpoints remain unsigned.
  - Evidence: backend/src/middleware.ts:113 (signed-session gate), backend/src/routes/auth.ts:46 (/bootstrap/status unsigned), backend/src/routes/auth.ts:70 (/login unsigned), backend/src/routes/auth.ts:141 (/session unsigned), frontend/src/api/client.ts:145, frontend/src/api/client.ts:155, frontend/src/api/client.ts:163 (client explicitly sends unsigned).
  - Impact: Replay-protection requirement is only partially met, weakening strict compliance with prompt security controls.
  - Minimum actionable fix: Define and enforce a consistent signing policy for all feasible API endpoints; document explicit exceptions (e.g., pre-auth bootstrap/login) and protect those via compensating controls.
- Severity: Medium
  - Conclusion: Rate limiting is not uniformly applied to all API/auth endpoints.
  - Brief rationale: Limiter exists for sign-in and signed-session flows, but not uniformly across all /api/auth/*.
  - Evidence: backend/src/middleware.ts:74, backend/src/middleware.ts:163, backend/src/app.ts:65, backend/src/routes/auth.ts:46, backend/src/routes/auth.ts:141.
  - Impact: Partial mismatch with strict “60 requests/min per IP” control language.
  - Minimum actionable fix: Add a global API limiter (60/min/IP baseline) and keep stricter endpoint-specific limits where needed.
- Severity: Medium
  - Conclusion: End-to-end delivery remains partially verified under current constraints.
  - Brief rationale: Canonical run/test path is Docker-based and not executed here.
  - Evidence: README.md:76, README.md:167, run_tests.sh:55.
  - Impact: Confidence in real deployment behavior is partial, not full.
  - Minimum actionable fix: Run docker compose up --build and ./run_tests.sh in an allowed environment and retain logs.
4. Security Summary
- authentication: Pass
  - Evidence: password policy, lockout, inactivity expiry, logout revocation are implemented (backend/src/security.ts:91, backend/src/services/auth-service.ts:107, backend/src/services/auth-service.ts:221, backend/src/services/auth-service.ts:498).
- route authorization: Partial Pass
  - Evidence: RBAC + signed-session checks are present (backend/src/middleware.ts:113, backend/src/middleware.ts:178, backend/src/app.ts:81), but signing/rate controls are not fully uniform across API/auth endpoints.
- object-level authorization: Pass
  - Evidence: member/face scoped checks and inbox ownership checks (backend/src/services/member-service.ts:149, backend/src/services/face-service.ts:338, backend/src/services/report-service.ts:570).
- tenant / user isolation: Partial Pass
  - Evidence: location/assignment scoping exists (backend/src/services/content-service.ts:26, backend/src/services/member-service.ts:63); strict tenant boundary model beyond location scope cannot be fully confirmed.
5. Test Sufficiency Summary
- Test Overview
  - whether unit tests exist: Yes (backend + frontend suites).
  - whether API / integration tests exist: Yes (backend/tests/routes.test.ts, Playwright specs in tests/e2e).
  - obvious test entry points if present: backend/package.json:test, frontend/package.json:test, npm run test:e2e, run_tests.sh.
- Core Coverage
  - happy path: covered
  - key failure paths: covered
  - security-critical coverage: partially covered
- Major Gaps
  - Docker-authoritative e2e/security regression path not executed in this audit.
  - No executed proof here that signing/rate-limit controls are uniformly enforced per strict prompt interpretation.
  - No executed production-like verification of IP allowlist behavior behind real network/proxy setup.
- Final Test Verdict
  - Partial Pass
6. Engineering Quality Summary
- Architecture remains credible and product-like: clean route/service separation, clear domain modules, non-trivial operational workflows.
- Validation, logging, and error handling are generally professional.
- Main confidence reducers are security-control consistency (signing/rate-limit uniformity) and unexecuted Docker runtime verification.
7. Next Actions
- 1) Enforce/document a strict API signing policy, with explicit pre-auth exceptions and compensating protections.
- 2) Apply uniform 60 req/min/IP throttling baseline across API entry points.
- 3) Execute Docker canonical verification (docker compose up --build, ./run_tests.sh) and archive evidence.
- 4) Add focused tests proving signing and rate-limit behavior on auth/session/status endpoints.

payload.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: README.md
@@
 - Signed request integrity with HMAC, nonce, and timestamp validation
@@
 ## Operational notes
@@
 - PIN re-entry shares the sign-in rate limit path and lockout policy to reduce brute-force exposure.
-- Session-state APIs use signed requests; reload-time restoration uses the server session endpoint and keeps the signing secret in memory only, while warm-lock resumes require PIN re-entry.
+- Request-signing policy:
+  - Signed (`x-sf-timestamp` + `x-sf-nonce` + `x-sf-signature`) is required for all authenticated business routes under `/api/self`, `/api/members`, `/api/faces`, `/api/content`, `/api/dashboards`, `/api/reports`, and `/api/admin`.
+  - Unsigned exceptions are limited to pre-auth/session-establishment flows: `/api/auth/bootstrap/status`, `/api/auth/bootstrap/admin`, `/api/auth/login`, `/api/auth/pin/reenter`, and `/api/auth/session`.
+  - Compensating controls for unsigned exceptions include IP allowlist enforcement, auth-route per-IP throttling, credential lockout policy, and workstation-binding cookie validation on `/api/auth/session`.
+- Session-state restoration uses the server session endpoint and keeps the signing secret in memory only, while warm-lock resumes require PIN re-entry.
*** End Patch

payload.message:
Good catch. I addressed these directly.

**What’s fixed now**
- Uniform auth-route throttling baseline is enforced at `60 req/min/IP` on `/api/auth/*`:
  - [app.ts](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend/src/app.ts)
  - [middleware.ts](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend/src/middleware.ts)
  - [config.ts](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend/src/config.ts)
  - [docker-compose.yml](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/docker-compose.yml)
- Signing-policy exceptions are now explicitly documented with compensating controls:
  - [README.md](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/README.md)
- Added focused auth/session/status coverage:
  - [routes.test.ts](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend/tests/routes.test.ts)
  - [config.test.ts](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend/tests/config.test.ts)

**Verification results**
- Backend tests: `110 passed`
- Frontend tests: `47 passed`
- Backend/frontend typechecks: passed
- Docker runtime and Playwright were executed and passed in seeded + clean-install bootstrap paths.

So the audit items about non-uniform auth throttling and missing signing-policy documentation are resolved in code/docs/tests.  
The only remaining “Partial Pass” interpretation would be if a reviewer insists that pre-auth endpoints must also be cryptographically signed, which is not feasible by design before session establishment.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text:
# Delivery Acceptance & Architecture Audit

## 1. Verdict
- **Partial Pass**

## 2. Scope and Verification Boundary
- Reviewed project documentation, backend/frontend architecture, route/service security controls, and automated test assets.
- Executed documented local verification commands (non-Docker):
  - `backend`: `npm test`, `npm run typecheck` (passed)
  - `frontend`: `npm test`, `npm run typecheck` (passed)
- Did **not** execute Docker-based runtime verification (`docker compose up --build`, `./run_tests.sh`) due review constraint disallowing container commands.
- Docker-based verification was required by the repository’s canonical run path (`README.md:77-107`) but not executed.
- Unconfirmed due boundary:
  - End-to-end runtime behavior of the full Docker stack (frontend + backend + primary/standby MySQL)
  - Playwright E2E execution against a live stack

## 3. Top Findings

### Finding 1
- **Severity:** High
- **Conclusion:** Replay protection is process-local and can be bypassed after server restart within the timestamp validity window.
- **Brief rationale:** Nonce replay state is held in an in-memory `Map` in middleware; it is not persisted/shared, so restart clears replay history while active sessions may still exist.
- **Evidence:**
  - In-memory nonce store construction and usage: `backend/src/middleware.ts:20-43`, `backend/src/middleware.ts:157`
  - Signature window remains valid for ±5 minutes: `backend/src/middleware.ts:152-155`
- **Impact:** A captured signed request could potentially be replayed after process restart/failover if timestamp remains valid and session cookie is still active.
- **Minimum actionable fix:** Persist nonce records with TTL per session in a shared durable store (e.g., MySQL/Redis) and enforce single-use nonce across restarts/instances.

### Finding 2
- **Severity:** Medium
- **Conclusion:** Canonical delivery runnability is only partially verified in this audit.
- **Brief rationale:** The documented authoritative startup/test path is Docker-first, but Docker execution was not performed under the review constraints.
- **Evidence:**
  - Docker-first startup documented as canonical: `README.md:77-107`
  - Canonical full verification path requires Docker in test script flow: `README.md:168-185`
  - Local non-Docker checks passed in this audit (`npm test`/`npm run typecheck` in both backend and frontend).
- **Impact:** Full deployment confidence is reduced; cross-service behavior in the actual intended runtime remains unconfirmed.
- **Minimum actionable fix:** Run and record `docker compose up --build` health checks plus `./run_tests.sh` results in the target acceptance environment.

## 4. Security Summary

- **authentication:** **Pass**
  - Evidence: password complexity enforced (`backend/src/security.ts:91-100`), account lockout policy (`backend/src/services/auth-service.ts:107-123`), session inactivity expiry (`backend/src/services/auth-service.ts:221-225`), explicit logout revocation (`backend/src/services/auth-service.ts:498-500`).

- **route authorization:** **Pass**
  - Evidence: route-level signed-session and role guards in app composition (`backend/src/app.ts:76-110`), plus `requireRole` enforcement (`backend/src/middleware.ts:188-201`).

- **object-level authorization:** **Pass**
  - Evidence: member object access checks (`backend/src/services/member-service.ts:149-168`), face record/member ownership checks (`backend/src/services/face-service.ts:338-357`, `backend/src/services/face-service.ts:719-730`), inbox download bound to requesting user (`backend/src/services/report-service.ts:569-577`).

- **tenant / user isolation:** **Partial Pass**
  - Evidence: location-based scope restrictions exist for content/member operations (`backend/src/services/content-service.ts:26-57`, `backend/src/services/member-service.ts:70-97`).
  - Boundary: no explicit multi-tenant model in schema/config; isolation is location-scoped rather than tenant-scoped.

## 5. Test Sufficiency Summary

- **Test Overview**
  - Unit tests exist: extensive backend/frontend Vitest suites (`backend/tests/*.test.ts`, `frontend/tests/*.spec.ts`).
  - API/integration tests exist: route/middleware tests via Supertest (`backend/tests/routes.test.ts`).
  - E2E entry points exist: Playwright scenarios (`tests/e2e/app.spec.ts`, `tests/e2e/bootstrap.spec.ts`) but were not executed in this audit.

- **Core Coverage**
  - happy path: **partial**
    - Evidence: broad happy-path route coverage in `backend/tests/routes.test.ts:654-850`; E2E happy flows authored in `tests/e2e/app.spec.ts:69-117`.
    - Boundary: E2E not executed against live Docker stack here.
  - key failure paths: **covered**
    - Evidence: explicit 401/403/423/429/validation/signature failures in `backend/tests/routes.test.ts:308-318`, `backend/tests/routes.test.ts:474-535`, `backend/tests/routes.test.ts:604-614`.
  - security-critical coverage: **partial**
    - Evidence: signed request checks, timestamp window, IP allowlist, role boundaries covered in route tests (`backend/tests/routes.test.ts:509-548`).
    - Gap: no test for replay resistance across process restart / multi-instance scenario.

- **Major Gaps**
  - Missing restart/failover replay test for nonce persistence semantics.
  - Docker-stack startup smoke test and readiness assertions were not executed in this audit context.
  - Real standby restore drill validation (not mocked) remains unconfirmed in this run.

- **Final Test Verdict**
  - **Partial Pass**

## 6. Engineering Quality Summary
- Overall structure is modular and product-like (separated routes/services/composables/components, clear domain slices).
- Server-side validation, logging, and error handling are consistently implemented, with encrypted-at-rest handling for sensitive data.
- Main material architecture risk is replay prevention durability (nonce store lifecycle), which reduces confidence for production-grade security behavior during restart/failover conditions.

## 7. Next Actions
1. Implement durable/shared nonce replay store with TTL and add restart/multi-instance replay tests.
2. Execute canonical Docker verification (`docker compose up --build`) and capture readiness evidence.
3. Run full canonical test pipeline (`./run_tests.sh`) in acceptance environment and archive outputs.
4. Add a dedicated security integration test proving replay is rejected after service restart.
5. Document tenant-isolation assumptions explicitly (single-tenant/location-scoped) to avoid deployment ambiguity.

payload.message:
# Delivery Acceptance & Architecture Audit

## 1. Verdict
- **Partial Pass**

## 2. Scope and Verification Boundary
- Reviewed project documentation, backend/frontend architecture, route/service security controls, and automated test assets.
- Executed documented local verification commands (non-Docker):
  - `backend`: `npm test`, `npm run typecheck` (passed)
  - `frontend`: `npm test`, `npm run typecheck` (passed)
- Did **not** execute Docker-based runtime verification (`docker compose up --build`, `./run_tests.sh`) due review constraint disallowing container commands.
- Docker-based verification was required by the repository’s canonical run path (`README.md:77-107`) but not executed.
- Unconfirmed due boundary:
  - End-to-end runtime behavior of the full Docker stack (frontend + backend + primary/standby MySQL)
  - Playwright E2E execution against a live stack

## 3. Top Findings

### Finding 1
- **Severity:** High
- **Conclusion:** Replay protection is process-local and can be bypassed after server restart within the timestamp validity window.
- **Brief rationale:** Nonce replay state is held in an in-memory `Map` in middleware; it is not persisted/shared, so restart clears replay history while active sessions may still exist.
- **Evidence:**
  - In-memory nonce store construction and usage: `backend/src/middleware.ts:20-43`, `backend/src/middleware.ts:157`
  - Signature window remains valid for ±5 minutes: `backend/src/middleware.ts:152-155`
- **Impact:** A captured signed request could potentially be replayed after process restart/failover if timestamp remains valid and session cookie is still active.
- **Minimum actionable fix:** Persist nonce records with TTL per session in a shared durable store (e.g., MySQL/Redis) and enforce single-use nonce across restarts/instances.

### Finding 2
- **Severity:** Medium
- **Conclusion:** Canonical delivery runnability is only partially verified in this audit.
- **Brief rationale:** The documented authoritative startup/test path is Docker-first, but Docker execution was not performed under the review constraints.
- **Evidence:**
  - Docker-first startup documented as canonical: `README.md:77-107`
  - Canonical full verification path requires Docker in test script flow: `README.md:168-185`
  - Local non-Docker checks passed in this audit (`npm test`/`npm run typecheck` in both backend and frontend).
- **Impact:** Full deployment confidence is reduced; cross-service behavior in the actual intended runtime remains unconfirmed.
- **Minimum actionable fix:** Run and record `docker compose up --build` health checks plus `./run_tests.sh` results in the target acceptance environment.

## 4. Security Summary

- **authentication:** **Pass**
  - Evidence: password complexity enforced (`backend/src/security.ts:91-100`), account lockout policy (`backend/src/services/auth-service.ts:107-123`), session inactivity expiry (`backend/src/services/auth-service.ts:221-225`), explicit logout revocation (`backend/src/services/auth-service.ts:498-500`).

- **route authorization:** **Pass**
  - Evidence: route-level signed-session and role guards in app composition (`backend/src/app.ts:76-110`), plus `requireRole` enforcement (`backend/src/middleware.ts:188-201`).

- **object-level authorization:** **Pass**
  - Evidence: member object access checks (`backend/src/services/member-service.ts:149-168`), face record/member ownership checks (`backend/src/services/face-service.ts:338-357`, `backend/src/services/face-service.ts:719-730`), inbox download bound to requesting user (`backend/src/services/report-service.ts:569-577`).

- **tenant / user isolation:** **Partial Pass**
  - Evidence: location-based scope restrictions exist for content/member operations (`backend/src/services/content-service.ts:26-57`, `backend/src/services/member-service.ts:70-97`).
  - Boundary: no explicit multi-tenant model in schema/config; isolation is location-scoped rather than tenant-scoped.

## 5. Test Sufficiency Summary

- **Test Overview**
  - Unit tests exist: extensive backend/frontend Vitest suites (`backend/tests/*.test.ts`, `frontend/tests/*.spec.ts`).
  - API/integration tests exist: route/middleware tests via Supertest (`backend/tests/routes.test.ts`).
  - E2E entry points exist: Playwright scenarios (`tests/e2e/app.spec.ts`, `tests/e2e/bootstrap.spec.ts`) but were not executed in this audit.

- **Core Coverage**
  - happy path: **partial**
    - Evidence: broad happy-path route coverage in `backend/tests/routes.test.ts:654-850`; E2E happy flows authored in `tests/e2e/app.spec.ts:69-117`.
    - Boundary: E2E not executed against live Docker stack here.
  - key failure paths: **covered**
    - Evidence: explicit 401/403/423/429/validation/signature failures in `backend/tests/routes.test.ts:308-318`, `backend/tests/routes.test.ts:474-535`, `backend/tests/routes.test.ts:604-614`.
  - security-critical coverage: **partial**
    - Evidence: signed request checks, timestamp window, IP allowlist, role boundaries covered in route tests (`backend/tests/routes.test.ts:509-548`).
    - Gap: no test for replay resistance across process restart / multi-instance scenario.

- **Major Gaps**
  - Missing restart/failover replay test for nonce persistence semantics.
  - Docker-stack startup smoke test and readiness assertions were not executed in this audit context.
  - Real standby restore drill validation (not mocked) remains unconfirmed in this run.

- **Final Test Verdict**
  - **Partial Pass**

## 6. Engineering Quality Summary
- Overall structure is modular and product-like (separated routes/services/composables/components, clear domain slices).
- Server-side validation, logging, and error handling are consistently implemented, with encrypted-at-rest handling for sensitive data.
- Main material architecture risk is replay prevention durability (nonce store lifecycle), which reduces confidence for production-grade security behavior during restart/failover conditions.

## 7. Next Actions
1. Implement durable/shared nonce replay store with TTL and add restart/multi-instance replay tests.
2. Execute canonical Docker verification (`docker compose up --build`) and capture readiness evidence.
3. Run full canonical test pipeline (`./run_tests.sh`) in acceptance environment and archive outputs.
4. Add a dedicated security integration test proving replay is rejected after service restart.
5. Document tenant-isolation assumptions explicitly (single-tenant/location-scoped) to avoid deployment ambiguity.

I can take this one all the way through. I’m implementing the high-severity replay fix now by moving nonce tracking from in-memory to a durable DB-backed store with TTL, then I’ll add regression tests (including replay-after-restart behavior simulation) and rerun tests.

 - Request-signing policy:
   - Signed (`x-sf-timestamp` + `x-sf-nonce` + `x-sf-signature`) is required for all authenticated business routes under `/api/self`, `/api/members`, `/api/faces`, `/api/content`, `/api/dashboards`, `/api/reports`, and `/api/admin`.
   - Unsigned exceptions are limited to pre-auth/session-establishment flows: `/api/auth/bootstrap/status`, `/api/auth/bootstrap/admin`, `/api/auth/login`, `/api/auth/pin/reenter`, and `/api/auth/session`.
-  - Compensating controls for unsigned exceptions include IP allowlist enforcement, auth-route per-IP throttling, credential lockout policy, and workstation-binding cookie validation on `/api/auth/session`.
+  - Compensating controls for unsigned exceptions include IP allowlist enforcement, auth-route per-IP throttling, credential lockout policy, and workstation-binding cookie validation on `/api/auth/session`.
+  - Replay protection for signed routes uses nonce single-use records stored in MySQL with TTL, so replay history survives process restarts and multi-instance routing.
*** End Patch

payload.message:
This audit’s two open items are now closed in code and verified.

- High finding fixed: nonce replay protection is now durable/shared (MySQL-backed), not process-local.
  - Added nonce table in schema: [schema.ts](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend/src/schema.ts)
  - Middleware now stores/checks nonce with TTL in DB and rejects duplicate nonce via `ER_DUP_ENTRY`: [middleware.ts](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend/src/middleware.ts)
  - App wiring updated to give middleware DB access: [app.ts](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend/src/app.ts)

- Added explicit regression coverage for restart/failover replay semantics:
  - New test simulates two app instances sharing nonce storage and confirms replay is rejected across “restart”: [routes.test.ts](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend/tests/routes.test.ts)

- Security policy docs updated:
  - Added explicit request-signing policy/exceptions and compensating controls, including durable nonce behavior: [README.md](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/README.md)

Verification I ran:
- `backend npm test` + `npm run typecheck`: passed (`111` backend tests)
- Docker live-stack checks: `docker compose up --build -d` + health checks passed
- Playwright on live stack:
  - `npm run test:e2e`: passed main app flows
  - warm-lock scenario: passed
  - clean-install bootstrap scenario: passed
- Stack teardown completed (`docker compose down --volumes --remove-orphans`)

So the replay-across-restart risk from the audit is now remediated and validated.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text:
Severity: Low
  - Conclusion: Repository contains generated/runtime artifacts and vendored dependencies, reducing delivery hygiene.
  - Brief rationale: Committed `node_modules` and generated report PDFs are unnecessary for source delivery and increase noise/risk of drift.
  - Evidence: directory listing includes `node_modules/` and many generated files under `shared-reports/` (e.g., `shared-reports/weekly-template-895155-1774786896567.pdf`).
  - Impact: Lower maintainability and review signal; larger repository footprint and potential stale artifact confusion.
  - Minimum actionable fix: Remove generated artifacts and vendored dependencies from VCS, keep `.gitignore` enforcement, and regenerate only in CI/runtime.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text:
- Severity: Medium
  - Conclusion: Biometric pipeline logic is overly concentrated in one large service file.
  - Brief rationale: Capture parsing, quality/liveness checks, dedup, encryption artifacts, persistence, and audit logic are tightly coupled.
  - Evidence: backend/src/services/face-service.ts:1 and backend/src/services/face-service.ts:833 (single large module).
  - Impact: Higher regression risk and lower maintainability for security-sensitive biometric changes.
  - Minimum actionable fix: Split into focused modules (validation/quality, liveness, dedup, persistence/audit) with dedicated tests per module.

payload.message:
- Severity: Medium
  - Conclusion: Biometric pipeline logic is overly concentrated in one large service file.
  - Brief rationale: Capture parsing, quality/liveness checks, dedup, encryption artifacts, persistence, and audit logic are tightly coupled.
  - Evidence: backend/src/services/face-service.ts:1 and backend/src/services/face-service.ts:833 (single large module).
  - Impact: Higher regression risk and lower maintainability for security-sensitive biometric changes.
  - Minimum actionable fix: Split into focused modules (validation/quality, liveness, dedup, persistence/audit) with dedicated tests per module.

I’ll do this as a safe structural refactor: split the biometric logic into focused internal modules while preserving runtime behavior and test outputs, then run backend tests to confirm zero functional regression. I’m starting by mapping `face-service.ts` into extraction boundaries.

payload.message:
Implemented this refactor and kept behavior intact.

**What I changed**
- Split biometric primitives out of the monolith:
  - [analysis.ts](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend/src/services/face/analysis.ts)  
    image decoding, quality scoring, trusted metadata/landmark extraction, artifact encryption/write
  - [liveness.ts](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend/src/services/face/liveness.ts)  
    trusted liveness score computation
  - [dedup.ts](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend/src/services/face/dedup.ts)  
    hash similarity helper
  - [types.ts](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend/src/services/face/types.ts)  
    shared face domain types
- Reduced [face-service.ts](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend/src/services/face-service.ts) to orchestration/persistence/audit logic, importing the extracted modules.

**Dedicated tests added per extracted module**
- [face-analysis.test.ts](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend/tests/face-analysis.test.ts)
- [face-liveness.test.ts](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend/tests/face-liveness.test.ts)
- [face-dedup.test.ts](E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend/tests/face-dedup.test.ts)

**Validation**
- Backend tests: passed (`116` tests total)
- Backend typecheck: passed

This directly addresses the maintainability/risk finding by separating validation/quality, liveness, dedup, and orchestration concerns without changing external behavior.
