settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text:
# Delivery Acceptance and Architecture Audit

## 1. Verdict
- **Partial Pass**

## 2. Scope and Verification Boundary
- Reviewed: repository documentation, backend/frontend architecture, auth/security middleware, RBAC/object-access paths, face lifecycle logic, reporting/ops modules, and automated test suites.
- Executed locally (non-Docker): `backend` and `frontend` dependency install, `npm test` with coverage, and `npm run typecheck` for both packages (all passed).
- Not executed: Docker-based runtime (`docker compose up --build`), Playwright E2E, and full MySQL-backed runtime flows, per review constraint prohibiting container commands.
- Docker-based verification required by project docs but not executed: Yes (README states Docker-first canonical path).
- Unconfirmed due boundary: end-to-end runtime behavior of full stack in canonical deployment path, including scheduled jobs under live MySQL/standby instances.

## 3. Top Findings

### Finding 1
- **Severity:** High
- **Conclusion:** API rate limiting is not consistently enforced across protected business routes, especially for invalid signed requests.
- **Brief rationale:** The global API limiter is attached only to `/api/auth`; other route families rely on `requireSignedSession`, where limiter execution occurs after signature validation. Invalid/missing signatures are rejected before rate-limit check.
- **Evidence:**
  - `backend/src/app.ts:64` to `backend/src/app.ts:75` applies `rateLimitedApi` only on `/api/auth`.
  - `backend/src/app.ts:76` to `backend/src/app.ts:110` mounts `/api/self`, `/api/members`, `/api/faces`, `/api/content`, `/api/dashboards`, `/api/reports`, `/api/admin` without `rateLimitedApi`.
  - `backend/src/middleware.ts:147` to `backend/src/middleware.ts:175` throws on missing/invalid signature before rate-limit call.
  - `backend/src/middleware.ts:177` to `backend/src/middleware.ts:181` performs limiter check only after successful signature verification.
- **Impact:** Does not fully satisfy the prompt requirement for 60 req/min per IP API access control; attackers can send high-volume invalid signed requests to protected endpoints without hitting the intended per-IP API throttle.
- **Minimum actionable fix:** Apply an IP-based API limiter middleware to all `/api/*` business routes before signature validation (or move limiter check to the start of `requireSignedSession`), while keeping login-specific limiter for auth endpoints.

### Finding 2
- **Severity:** Medium
- **Conclusion:** Canonical runtime verification is currently unconfirmed in this audit.
- **Brief rationale:** The delivered docs define Docker Compose as authoritative startup/verification path, but Docker execution is out of bounds for this review.
- **Evidence:**
  - `README.md:77` to `README.md:83` defines `docker compose up --build` as canonical startup.
  - `README.md:168` to `README.md:185` defines `./run_tests.sh` flow including Docker startup and E2E.
  - Verification boundary: Docker commands were not run by rule.
- **Impact:** Delivery runnability in the claimed production-like path is not disproven, but not independently confirmed in this audit.
- **Minimum actionable fix:** Run documented Docker startup and E2E (`docker compose up --build`, then `./run_tests.sh`) in a permitted environment and attach results/logs to delivery evidence.

### Finding 3
- **Severity:** Medium
- **Conclusion:** Security-critical test coverage is strong at unit/integration level, but real deployment-path security behavior remains only partially confirmed.
- **Brief rationale:** Local backend/frontend tests pass, including many auth/signing/RBAC scenarios, but containerized E2E and real DB-backed replay/rate-limit behavior were not executed here.
- **Evidence:**
  - Local command output: backend `116` tests passed with coverage; frontend `47` tests passed with coverage; both typechecks passed.
  - `tests/e2e/app.spec.ts:69` onward contains meaningful E2E scenarios, but these were not run due Docker boundary.
- **Impact:** Confidence is good for module-level correctness; confidence is lower for full-stack behavior under the documented runtime path.
- **Minimum actionable fix:** Add one non-mocked integration test that asserts per-IP throttling on protected routes for invalid signature requests, and execute Docker E2E in CI artifacted runs.

## 4. Security Summary
- **authentication:** **Pass**
  - Evidence: Password policy and PIN policy enforced (`backend/src/security.ts:91`, `backend/src/security.ts:103`), session expiry and revocation (`backend/src/services/auth-service.ts:214`, `backend/src/services/auth-service.ts:498`), lockout logic (`backend/src/services/auth-service.ts:107` to `backend/src/services/auth-service.ts:123`).
- **route authorization:** **Partial Pass**
  - Evidence: Role-gated route families in `backend/src/app.ts:81` to `backend/src/app.ts:110`; however, API rate-limit control is inconsistently applied as detailed in Finding 1.
- **object-level authorization:** **Pass**
  - Evidence: Member access checks in `backend/src/services/member-service.ts:149` to `backend/src/services/member-service.ts:169`; face record/member scoping checks in `backend/src/services/face-service.ts:30` to `backend/src/services/face-service.ts:49` and at action entry points.
- **tenant / user isolation:** **Cannot Confirm**
  - Evidence/boundary: No explicit multi-tenant model in prompt-implementation surface; user-level isolation checks exist, but tenant isolation semantics are not separately defined/tested.

## 5. Test Sufficiency Summary
- **Test Overview**
  - Unit tests exist: Yes (backend + frontend Vitest suites).
  - API/integration tests exist: Yes (`backend/tests/routes.test.ts`, service tests using stubs/mocks).
  - Obvious test entry points: `backend npm test`, `frontend npm test`, Playwright specs in `tests/e2e`.
- **Core Coverage**
  - Happy path: **Partially Covered**
    - Evidence: strong module and route tests; E2E happy path exists (`tests/e2e/app.spec.ts`) but not executed in this audit.
  - Key failure paths: **Covered**
    - Evidence: route/security tests include 401/403/429-style behavior (e.g., `backend/tests/routes.test.ts`, `backend/tests/security.test.ts`).
  - Security-critical coverage: **Partially Covered**
    - Evidence: signature, nonce, RBAC, workstation-binding scenarios are tested; missing explicit assertion that invalid signature requests on protected routes are rate-limited.
- **Major Gaps**
  - Add integration test proving per-IP API rate limiting applies to protected routes even when signatures are invalid.
  - Execute and publish Docker-path Playwright results for the canonical runtime.
  - Add one DB-backed integration check for nonce replay persistence/cleanup behavior under real MySQL.
- **Final Test Verdict:** **Partial Pass**

## 6. Engineering Quality Summary
- The codebase is structured as a real product (modular backend services/routes, Vue composables/components, schema + ops/reporting + tests) rather than a single-file demo.
- Validation, typed contracts, logging, and error handling are generally professional and consistent.
- Major maintainability risk observed is concentrated in cross-cutting security control placement (rate limiting location/order), not in overall project decomposition.

## 7. Next Actions
- 1) Fix API-wide rate limiting placement so protected routes are throttled per-IP before signature validation failure paths.
- 2) Re-run canonical Docker verification (`docker compose up --build` + `./run_tests.sh`) and attach outputs as delivery evidence.
- 3) Add regression tests for protected-route throttling on invalid signatures.
- 4) Add one real-MySQL integration test for nonce replay handling across repeated requests.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text:
# 1. Verdict
Partial Pass

# 2. Scope and Verification Boundary
- Reviewed: frontend implementation under `frontend/src`, frontend test suite under `frontend/tests`, root runtime/testing docs in `README.md`, and E2E test presence in `tests/e2e`.
- Excluded by rule: any content under `./.tmp/` (none was used as evidence).
- Runtime executed (non-Docker):
  - `npm test` in `frontend` (passed: 6 files, 47 tests)
  - `npm run typecheck` in `frontend` (passed)
  - `npm run dev` in `frontend` (Vite started on `http://localhost:5173` before timeout)
- Not executed:
  - Any Docker command (`docker`, `docker compose`, etc.) per instruction.
  - Full integrated E2E run (`npm run test:e2e`) because documented canonical runtime requires backend+DB stack and repo documentation defines Docker as authoritative path.
- Docker-based verification was required for canonical full-stack verification per `README.md:77-83`, `README.md:168-185`, but was not executed by rule.
- Unconfirmed due boundary: backend-enforced controls (AES-at-rest, lockout/session invalidation internals, HMAC replay window enforcement, IP allowlist/rate limit execution), and full cross-service runtime behavior.

# 3. Top Findings

1) **Severity: High**  
**Conclusion:** Canonical run/verification path is Docker-only, so full delivery verification is bounded in this review.  
**Brief rationale:** Project docs define Docker as the authoritative startup/test path; non-Docker verification confirms frontend app health but not full prompt lifecycle.  
**Evidence:** `README.md:77-83`, `README.md:168-185`, `README.md:186-207`; runtime evidence from this review: frontend `npm test` pass, `npm run typecheck` pass, `npm run dev` started.  
**Impact:** End-to-end acceptance for the full business flow remains partially unverified in this inspection context.  
**Minimum actionable fix:** Add a documented non-Docker local verification path for frontend+mocked backend or lightweight local DB option, and mark exactly which acceptance flows it covers.

2) **Severity: High**  
**Conclusion:** 4-6 digit PIN constraint is not consistently enforced in frontend PIN entry/setup flows.  
**Brief rationale:** Prompt requires optional quick re-entry PIN with explicit 4-6 digits; warm-lock modal has only `maxlength=6`, while other PIN inputs lack numeric/length guards.  
**Evidence:** `frontend/src/components/WarmLockModal.vue:23-27` (inputmode numeric + maxlength only), `frontend/src/components/AuthShell.vue:47` (PIN input without length/numeric constraints), `frontend/src/components/OverviewView.vue:29-37` (PIN setup input/button without 4-6 digit validation).  
**Impact:** Users can submit invalid PIN formats from UI; increases auth-flow friction and shifts all feedback to server-side rejection.  
**Minimum actionable fix:** Enforce `/^\d{4,6}$/` in UI before submit for PIN setup and PIN re-entry paths, with inline validation messages and disabled submit until valid.

3) **Severity: Medium**  
**Conclusion:** Face image import UI advertises constraints (JPG/PNG, max 5 MB), but client-side checks are incomplete and minimum resolution feedback is absent pre-submit.  
**Brief rationale:** Prompt requires import/capture constraints and immediate quality feedback; UI only constrains file picker MIME and reads file directly.  
**Evidence:** `frontend/src/components/CameraCapturePanel.vue:99-100` (claims JPG/PNG up to 5 MB), `frontend/src/components/CameraCapturePanel.vue:114` (accept MIME only), `frontend/src/components/CameraCapturePanel.vue:72-87` (no file-size or resolution rejection).  
**Impact:** Operators may proceed with invalid files and only fail later, reducing workflow reliability at front desk capture station.  
**Minimum actionable fix:** Add pre-upload checks for type, `file.size <= 5MB`, and decoded dimensions `>= 640x480`, with immediate user-facing error state.

4) **Severity: Medium**  
**Conclusion:** Frontend package lacks build/preview command, weakening acceptance clarity for "start/run/build/preview" expectations.  
**Brief rationale:** `dev`, `test`, and `typecheck` exist, but no explicit production build/preview script for frontend acceptance packaging.  
**Evidence:** `frontend/package.json:6-10` (no `build`/`preview` scripts).  
**Impact:** Delivery readiness and artifact reproducibility are less explicit for reviewers/operators.  
**Minimum actionable fix:** Add `build` and `preview` scripts and document them in README with expected outputs.

# 4. Security Summary
- **authentication / login-state handling:** **Partial Pass**  
  Evidence: Auth store keeps `sessionSecret` in memory and clears on logout (`frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:69`, `frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:105-113`, `frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:253-256`); warm-lock flow exists (`frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:271-288`). PIN format validation is incomplete (see Finding #2).

- **frontend route protection / route guards:** **Partial Pass**  
  Evidence: No `vue-router` route guards are present; access control is implemented via store-level `canAccessView` and conditional rendering (`frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:40-64`, `frontend/src/App.vue:162-166`, `frontend/src/App.vue:350-486`). Works for in-app nav but is not URL-route guard based.

- **page-level / feature-level access control:** **Pass**  
  Evidence: Role-gated nav and view checks for Members/Coach/Admin features (`frontend/src/App.vue:132-143`, `frontend/src/App.vue:350-486`, `frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:53-61`). Tests also assert coach/admin boundaries (`tests/e2e/app.spec.ts:119-130`, `tests/e2e/app.spec.ts:145-150`).

- **sensitive information exposure:** **Partial Pass**  
  Evidence: No `console` logging in frontend source (`grep` on `frontend/src` found none); phone is displayed as masked field (`frontend/src/components/MembersView.vue:63`, `frontend/src/types.ts:24-25`). However user context is persisted to localStorage for warm-lock resume (`frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:81-87`, `frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:277-282`).

- **cache / state isolation after switching users:** **Pass**  
  Evidence: Feature state reset on user-id change and session loss (`frontend/src/App.vue:265-290`, `frontend/src/App.vue:153-160`); auth store forces unauthorized view fallback (`frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:122-126`, `frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:263-269`). Covered by app/auth tests (`frontend/tests/App.spec.ts:80-229`, `frontend/tests/auth-store.spec.ts:242-279`).

# 5. Test Sufficiency Summary
## Test Overview
- Unit tests: **Yes** (`frontend/tests/date-utils.spec.ts`, `frontend/tests/api-client.spec.ts`, `frontend/tests/auth-store.spec.ts`)
- Component tests: **Yes** (`frontend/tests/components.spec.ts`)
- Page/route integration tests: **Partial** (App-level integration via `frontend/tests/App.spec.ts`; no URL route integration since router not used)
- E2E tests: **Yes, present but not executed in this review** (`tests/e2e/app.spec.ts`, `tests/e2e/bootstrap.spec.ts`; entrypoint `npm run test:e2e` in `package.json:5-7`)
- Obvious test entry points: `frontend/package.json:8-10` (`npm test`, `npm run typecheck`), root `package.json:5-7` (`npm run test:e2e`)

## Core Coverage
- happy path: **covered**  
  Evidence: composables/app tests cover member enrollment, content, dashboards/reports, auth transitions (`frontend/tests/composables.spec.ts`, `frontend/tests/App.spec.ts`)
- key failure paths: **partial**  
  Evidence: date validation, duplicate-submit guards, API error paths are tested (`frontend/tests/composables.spec.ts:126-148`, `frontend/tests/composables.spec.ts:237-257`, `frontend/tests/composables.spec.ts:344-373`), but PIN format and file constraint validation are not covered.
- security-critical coverage: **partial**  
  Evidence: auth store and role boundary behavior tested (`frontend/tests/auth-store.spec.ts`, `tests/e2e/app.spec.ts:119-150`, `tests/e2e/app.spec.ts:223-295`), but no explicit frontend tests asserting PIN 4-6 policy or file-size/resolution enforcement.

## Major Gaps (top 3)
1. Missing tests for PIN input policy enforcement (4-6 numeric digits) across setup + re-entry UI.
2. Missing tests for client-side file constraint handling (size/resolution/type rejection UX).
3. E2E suite not demonstrated in this review boundary (Docker canonical runtime not executed).

## Final Test Verdict
Partial Pass

# 6. Engineering Quality Summary
- Overall structure is credible and maintainable: clear split across store, API client, feature composables, and presentational components (`frontend/src/stores`, `frontend/src/api`, `frontend/src/composables`, `frontend/src/components`).
- State management and anti-duplication guards are consistently applied in async actions (`useMembers`, `useFaceOps`, `useContentAnalytics`, `useDashboardsReports`).
- Role-based feature gating is centralized and reused (`frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:40-64`).
- Material maintainability concern: no production build/preview workflow in frontend scripts (Finding #4), which weakens release-readiness clarity.

# 7. Visual and Interaction Summary
- Visual hierarchy and module separation are generally clear (auth shell, nav rail, section cards, metric cards), with consistent spacing and style tokens (`frontend/src/styles.css`, component library usage).
- Interaction feedback is present for loading/success/error and disabled states in key workflows (member creation, report generation/download, backup/recovery).
- Material interaction gap: constraint-specific immediate validation is incomplete for PIN and image import requirements (Findings #2 and #3).

# 8. Next Actions
1. Enforce and test 4-6 numeric PIN validation in all PIN entry/setup flows (highest security/prompt-fit unblocker).
2. Add client-side image file constraint checks (JPG/PNG, <=5MB, >=640x480) with immediate UX feedback and tests.
3. Add `build`/`preview` scripts for frontend and document expected acceptance verification outputs.
4. Provide a non-Docker local verification profile for reviewers, or explicitly map which acceptance checks are Docker-only.
5. Run and publish E2E results in an environment that satisfies the documented canonical runtime.

  - Full integrated E2E run (`npm run test:e2e`) because documented canonical runtime requires backend+DB stack and repo documentation defines Docker as authoritative path.
- Docker-based verification was required for canonical full-stack verification per `README.md:77-83`, `README.md:168-185`, but was not executed by rule.
- Unconfirmed due boundary: backend-enforced controls (AES-at-rest, lockout/session invalidation internals, HMAC replay window enforcement, IP allowlist/rate limit execution), and full cross-service runtime behavior.

# 3. Top Findings

1) **Severity: High**  
**Conclusion:** Canonical run/verification path is Docker-only, so full delivery verification is bounded in this review.  
**Brief rationale:** Project docs define Docker as the authoritative startup/test path; non-Docker verification confirms frontend app health but not full prompt lifecycle.  
**Evidence:** `README.md:77-83`, `README.md:168-185`, `README.md:186-207`; runtime evidence from this review: frontend `npm test` pass, `npm run typecheck` pass, `npm run dev` started.  
**Impact:** End-to-end acceptance for the full business flow remains partially unverified in this inspection context.  
**Minimum actionable fix:** Add a documented non-Docker local verification path for frontend+mocked backend or lightweight local DB option, and mark exactly which acceptance flows it covers.

2) **Severity: High**  
**Conclusion:** 4-6 digit PIN constraint is not consistently enforced in frontend PIN entry/setup flows.  
**Brief rationale:** Prompt requires optional quick re-entry PIN with explicit 4-6 digits; warm-lock modal has only `maxlength=6`, while other PIN inputs lack numeric/length guards.  
**Evidence:** `frontend/src/components/WarmLockModal.vue:23-27` (inputmode numeric + maxlength only), `frontend/src/components/AuthShell.vue:47` (PIN input without length/numeric constraints), `frontend/src/components/OverviewView.vue:29-37` (PIN setup input/button without 4-6 digit validation).  
**Impact:** Users can submit invalid PIN formats from UI; increases auth-flow friction and shifts all feedback to server-side rejection.  
**Minimum actionable fix:** Enforce `/^\d{4,6}$/` in UI before submit for PIN setup and PIN re-entry paths, with inline validation messages and disabled submit until valid.

3) **Severity: Medium**  
**Conclusion:** Face image import UI advertises constraints (JPG/PNG, max 5 MB), but client-side checks are incomplete and minimum resolution feedback is absent pre-submit.  
**Brief rationale:** Prompt requires import/capture constraints and immediate quality feedback; UI only constrains file picker MIME and reads file directly.  
**Evidence:** `frontend/src/components/CameraCapturePanel.vue:99-100` (claims JPG/PNG up to 5 MB), `frontend/src/components/CameraCapturePanel.vue:114` (accept MIME only), `frontend/src/components/CameraCapturePanel.vue:72-87` (no file-size or resolution rejection).  
**Impact:** Operators may proceed with invalid files and only fail later, reducing workflow reliability at front desk capture station.  
**Minimum actionable fix:** Add pre-upload checks for type, `file.size <= 5MB`, and decoded dimensions `>= 640x480`, with immediate user-facing error state.

4) **Severity: Medium**  
**Conclusion:** Frontend package lacks build/preview command, weakening acceptance clarity for "start/run/build/preview" expectations.  
**Brief rationale:** `dev`, `test`, and `typecheck` exist, but no explicit production build/preview script for frontend acceptance packaging.  
**Evidence:** `frontend/package.json:6-10` (no `build`/`preview` scripts).  
**Impact:** Delivery readiness and artifact reproducibility are less explicit for reviewers/operators.  
**Minimum actionable fix:** Add `build` and `preview` scripts and document them in README with expected outputs.

# 4. Security Summary
- **authentication / login-state handling:** **Partial Pass**  
  Evidence: Auth store keeps `sessionSecret` in memory and clears on logout (`frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:69`, `frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:105-113`, `frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:253-256`); warm-lock flow exists (`frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:271-288`). PIN format validation is incomplete (see Finding #2).

- **frontend route protection / route guards:** **Partial Pass**  
  Evidence: No `vue-router` route guards are present; access control is implemented via store-level `canAccessView` and conditional rendering (`frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:40-64`, `frontend/src/App.vue:162-166`, `frontend/src/App.vue:350-486`). Works for in-app nav but is not URL-route guard based.

- **page-level / feature-level access control:** **Pass**  
  Evidence: Role-gated nav and view checks for Members/Coach/Admin features (`frontend/src/App.vue:132-143`, `frontend/src/App.vue:350-486`, `frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:53-61`). Tests also assert coach/admin boundaries (`tests/e2e/app.spec.ts:119-130`, `tests/e2e/app.spec.ts:145-150`).

- **sensitive information exposure:** **Partial Pass**  
  Evidence: No `console` logging in frontend source (`grep` on `frontend/src` found none); phone is displayed as masked field (`frontend/src/components/MembersView.vue:63`, `frontend/src/types.ts:24-25`). However user context is persisted to localStorage for warm-lock resume (`frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:81-87`, `frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:277-282`).

- **cache / state isolation after switching users:** **Pass**  
  Evidence: Feature state reset on user-id change and session loss (`frontend/src/App.vue:265-290`, `frontend/src/App.vue:153-160`); auth store forces unauthorized view fallback (`frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:122-126`, `frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:263-269`). Covered by app/auth tests (`frontend/tests/App.spec.ts:80-229`, `frontend/tests/auth-store.spec.ts:242-279`).

# 5. Test Sufficiency Summary
## Test Overview
- Unit tests: **Yes** (`frontend/tests/date-utils.spec.ts`, `frontend/tests/api-client.spec.ts`, `frontend/tests/auth-store.spec.ts`)
- Component tests: **Yes** (`frontend/tests/components.spec.ts`)
- Page/route integration tests: **Partial** (App-level integration via `frontend/tests/App.spec.ts`; no URL route integration since router not used)
- E2E tests: **Yes, present but not executed in this review** (`tests/e2e/app.spec.ts`, `tests/e2e/bootstrap.spec.ts`; entrypoint `npm run test:e2e` in `package.json:5-7`)
- Obvious test entry points: `frontend/package.json:8-10` (`npm test`, `npm run typecheck`), root `package.json:5-7` (`npm run test:e2e`)

## Core Coverage
- happy path: **covered**  
  Evidence: composables/app tests cover member enrollment, content, dashboards/reports, auth transitions (`frontend/tests/composables.spec.ts`, `frontend/tests/App.spec.ts`)
- key failure paths: **partial**  
  Evidence: date validation, duplicate-submit guards, API error paths are tested (`frontend/tests/composables.spec.ts:126-148`, `frontend/tests/composables.spec.ts:237-257`, `frontend/tests/composables.spec.ts:344-373`), but PIN format and file constraint validation are not covered.
- security-critical coverage: **partial**  
  Evidence: auth store and role boundary behavior tested (`frontend/tests/auth-store.spec.ts`, `tests/e2e/app.spec.ts:119-150`, `tests/e2e/app.spec.ts:223-295`), but no explicit frontend tests asserting PIN 4-6 policy or file-size/resolution enforcement.

## Major Gaps (top 3)
1. Missing tests for PIN input policy enforcement (4-6 numeric digits) across setup + re-entry UI.
2. Missing tests for client-side file constraint handling (size/resolution/type rejection UX).
3. E2E suite not demonstrated in this review boundary (Docker canonical runtime not executed).

## Final Test Verdict
Partial Pass

# 6. Engineering Quality Summary
- Overall structure is credible and maintainable: clear split across store, API client, feature composables, and presentational components (`frontend/src/stores`, `frontend/src/api`, `frontend/src/composables`, `frontend/src/components`).
- State management and anti-duplication guards are consistently applied in async actions (`useMembers`, `useFaceOps`, `useContentAnalytics`, `useDashboardsReports`).
- Role-based feature gating is centralized and reused (`frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:40-64`).
- Material maintainability concern: no production build/preview workflow in frontend scripts (Finding #4), which weakens release-readiness clarity.

# 7. Visual and Interaction Summary
- Visual hierarchy and module separation are generally clear (auth shell, nav rail, section cards, metric cards), with consistent spacing and style tokens (`frontend/src/styles.css`, component library usage).
- Interaction feedback is present for loading/success/error and disabled states in key workflows (member creation, report generation/download, backup/recovery).
- Material interaction gap: constraint-specific immediate validation is incomplete for PIN and image import requirements (Findings #2 and #3).

# 8. Next Actions
1. Enforce and test 4-6 numeric PIN validation in all PIN entry/setup flows (highest security/prompt-fit unblocker).
2. Add client-side image file constraint checks (JPG/PNG, <=5MB, >=640x480) with immediate UX feedback and tests.
3. Add `build`/`preview` scripts for frontend and document expected acceptance verification outputs.
4. Provide a non-Docker local verification profile for reviewers, or explicitly map which acceptance checks are Docker-only.
5. Run and publish E2E results in an environment that satisfies the documented canonical runtime.

I’ll implement the frontend fixes from this audit now: PIN validation across all PIN flows, client-side image import constraints with immediate errors, add `build/preview` scripts, and update tests/docs accordingly. Then I’ll rerun frontend tests and typecheck.

# SentinelFit Operations Platform

SentinelFit is a fully on-prem operations platform for gyms and training studios. This implementation delivers the planned vertical slices across authentication, member enrollment, biometric governance, coaching content, analytics, dashboarding, scheduled reporting, observability, and backup/recovery using a Docker-first runtime.

The implementation benchmark used for this repository is [temp/final_plan_of_action.md](/E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/temp/final_plan_of_action.md).

## Stack

- Frontend: Vue 3, TypeScript, Pinia, Tailwind CSS, TensorFlow.js, Chart.js
- Backend: Express, TypeScript, Node.js, node-cron
- Database: MySQL 8
- Runtime: Docker Compose, fully offline/on-prem, no external API dependencies

## Implemented modules

- Docker-first runtime with frontend, backend, primary MySQL, and standby MySQL
- Password login, server-bound same-workstation PIN re-entry, warm-lock after inactivity, session expiry, and account-wide lockout handling
- Signed request integrity with HMAC, nonce, and timestamp validation
- Inclusive RBAC for Member, Coach, and Administrator
- Member enrollment, coach assignment, masked phone handling, administrator/coach consent updates, member self-service face consent, and operator-entered member passwords with no shared UI default
- Face enrollment with camera capture or image import, encrypted biometric artifacts, client-side quality guidance, pre-submit duplicate warnings, server-issued timed liveness challenges, server-derived face/liveness validation, dedup, versioning, deactivation, and immutable biometric audit records
- Coach content publishing and analytics by station/location/date with MM/DD/YYYY filters, inline chart drill-down, and real view/search event capture
- Admin dashboard layout persistence, configurable widget palette, and reusable report templates
- Scheduled/offline reporting with subscriptions, inbox delivery, actionable downloads, and shared-folder output
- Admin console for logs, alerts, latency/error metrics, backup, and dry-run restore
- Encrypted local key vault with key version metadata and rotation support
- Unit, integration-style, and Playwright end-to-end verification

## Repository layout

- [frontend](/E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/frontend): Vue application
- [backend](/E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend): Express API and background services
- [tests/e2e](/E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/tests/e2e): Playwright end-to-end tests
- [temp](/E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/temp): planning notes and implementation benchmark

## Prerequisites

- Docker Desktop with Compose
- Node.js 20+
- npm 10+
- Bash-compatible shell for `run_tests.sh`

## Configuration

1. Run with defaults directly via `docker compose up --build` for local/offline bootstrap on a fresh machine.
2. Optionally provide a `.env` file to override defaults for ports, credentials, shared-folder paths, or data directories.

Important environment variables:

- `MYSQL_DATABASE`
- `MYSQL_USER`
- `MYSQL_PASSWORD`
- `MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD`
- `MYSQL_PORT`
- `MYSQL_PORT_BIND`
- `MYSQL_HOST`
- `MYSQL_STANDBY_HOST`
- `MYSQL_STANDBY_PORT`
- `MYSQL_STANDBY_PORT_BIND`
- `BACKEND_PORT`
- `BACKEND_NODE_ENV`
- `BACKEND_ALLOWED_ORIGINS`
- `BACKEND_DATA_DIR`
- `BACKEND_REPORTS_SHARED_PATH`
- `REPORTS_SHARED_HOST_PATH`
- `BACKEND_IP_ALLOWLIST`
- `BACKEND_API_RATE_LIMIT_PER_MINUTE`
- `BACKEND_DEMO_SEED_USERS`
- `BACKEND_KEY_VAULT_MASTER_KEY`
- `FRONTEND_PORT`
- `VITE_API_BASE_URL`

The default configuration allows both `localhost` and `127.0.0.1` frontend origins so browser verification remains stable in local and Docker-driven access paths.
The shipped allowlist defaults cover local and private-LAN ranges so the offline deployment path does not start in an allow-all state.
The Docker stack now binds report delivery to `REPORTS_SHARED_HOST_PATH` on the host so exported files are directly visible outside containers.

## Run with Docker

The canonical startup command is:

```bash
docker compose up --build
```

Services:

- Frontend: [http://localhost:5173](http://localhost:5173)
- Backend live health: [http://localhost:3000/health/live](http://localhost:3000/health/live)
- Backend readiness: [http://localhost:3000/health/ready](http://localhost:3000/health/ready)
- Primary MySQL: `localhost:3306`
- Standby MySQL: `localhost:3307`

Shared report delivery:

- Host path: `${REPORTS_SHARED_HOST_PATH}` (default `./shared-reports`)
- Container path: `${BACKEND_REPORTS_SHARED_PATH}` (default `/shared/reports`)
- For Windows UNC/SMB shares, set `REPORTS_SHARED_HOST_PATH` to an absolute host-mounted path available to Docker Desktop.

## Quick start (new machine)

1. Install Docker Desktop (or Docker Engine + Compose plugin) and start Docker.
2. Open this repository directory.
3. Run:

```bash
docker compose up --build
```

4. Wait until services are healthy, then open [http://localhost:5173](http://localhost:5173).
5. If `BACKEND_DEMO_SEED_USERS=false` (default), complete the one-time â€œFirst administrator setupâ€.
6. To stop:

```bash
docker compose down
```

Notes:
- No `.env` file is required for local startup; Compose defaults are provided.
- Ensure ports `5173`, `3000`, `3306`, and `3307` are free on the host.

## Troubleshooting

- Docker daemon not running:
  Start Docker Desktop and retry `docker compose up --build`.

- Port already in use:
  Find and stop conflicting processes, or override bind ports in `.env` (for example `FRONTEND_PORT`, `BACKEND_PORT`, `MYSQL_PORT_BIND`, `MYSQL_STANDBY_PORT_BIND`).

- Backend cannot connect to MySQL after config changes:
  Recreate clean volumes:
  ```bash
  docker compose down --volumes --remove-orphans
  docker compose up --build
  ```

- Health endpoint not ready:
  Check logs:
  ```bash
  docker compose logs backend
  docker compose logs mysql
  ```
  Backend readiness endpoint: [http://localhost:3000/health/ready](http://localhost:3000/health/ready)

- UI loads but login/bootstrap fails:
  Confirm backend is reachable at [http://localhost:3000/health/live](http://localhost:3000/health/live) and that station token input is set in the UI.

## Demo seed accounts

Demo users are only created when `BACKEND_DEMO_SEED_USERS=true`.

- `admin` / `Admin12345!X`
- `coach` / `Coach12345!X`
- `member` / `Member12345!X`

The default non-demo runtime leaves seed users disabled so a clean deployment does not start with publicly documented credentials.

## First-boot administrator setup

When `BACKEND_DEMO_SEED_USERS=false`, SentinelFit presents a one-time administrator bootstrap flow instead of relying on pre-seeded credentials.

1. Start the stack with `docker compose up --build`
2. Open [http://localhost:5173](http://localhost:5173)
3. Complete the "First administrator setup" form
4. The created administrator is signed in immediately and the bootstrap path is closed once an administrator exists

## Testing

The canonical unified test entrypoint is:

```bash
./run_tests.sh
```

`run_tests.sh` performs:

- root dependency install
- backend dependency install and coverage-checked tests
- frontend dependency install and coverage-checked tests
- Playwright browser install
- `docker compose up --build -d`
- readiness wait for frontend and backend
- Playwright end-to-end tests
- clean-install Docker restart with demo seeds disabled
- first-boot Playwright bootstrap verification

Local verification commands are still useful, but Dockerized behavior is authoritative.
Before running them directly, install package-local dependencies once:

```bash
cd backend && npm ci
cd ../frontend && npm ci
cd ..
```

Then run:

```bash
cd backend && npm test
cd backend && npm run typecheck
cd frontend && npm test
cd frontend && npm run typecheck
npm run test:e2e
```

`npm run test:e2e` assumes frontend and backend services are already running and reachable on `127.0.0.1:5173` and `127.0.0.1:3000`. For a self-contained run, use `./run_tests.sh` (Docker canonical path).
Local non-Docker checks validate source-level behavior and contracts, but deployment/runtime readiness is only guaranteed by the Docker-first path.

## Operational notes

- Sensitive fields are encrypted with AES-256 via a local key vault whose key material is envelope-encrypted at rest with `BACKEND_KEY_VAULT_MASTER_KEY`.
- Key metadata is stored in MySQL and historical keys remain available for decrypt-on-read.
- `KEY_VAULT_MASTER_KEY` defaults to `AUTO_GENERATE`, which creates a per-environment master key on first boot and stores it under the backend data directory.
- `MYSQL_USER`, `MYSQL_PASSWORD`, and `MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD` have local bootstrap defaults for one-command startup; override them for production/shared deployments.
- Persisted session secrets are encrypted and persisted session identifiers are stored as non-reusable hashes.
- Biometric audit records are append-only and protected by MySQL triggers.
- Stored biometric artifacts are encrypted before being written to disk.
- Timed liveness challenges require the prompted head-turn capture to occur within the enforced server-side window.
- Final face-in-frame and liveness acceptance are derived from image content on the server; client landmark clicks are guidance-only and never the source of truth.
- The current biometric dedup/liveness layer uses lightweight local image heuristics suitable for offline baseline validation; high-assurance deployments should replace this with a stronger local face pipeline.
- Face duplicate checks can be previewed before final enrollment submission so operators see warnings during capture review, not only after commit.
- Preview-only dedup checks do not persist orphaned biometric artifacts to disk.
- PIN re-entry is only valid when an active warm session already exists and the browser presents the server-issued workstation binding cookie; the station token remains attribution metadata, not the security proof.
- PIN re-entry shares the sign-in rate limit path and lockout policy to reduce brute-force exposure.
- Request-signing policy:
  - Signed (`x-sf-timestamp` + `x-sf-nonce` + `x-sf-signature`) is required for all authenticated business routes under `/api/self`, `/api/members`, `/api/faces`, `/api/content`, `/api/dashboards`, `/api/reports`, and `/api/admin`.
  - Unsigned exceptions are limited to pre-auth/session-establishment flows: `/api/auth/bootstrap/status`, `/api/auth/bootstrap/admin`, `/api/auth/login`, `/api/auth/pin/reenter`, and `/api/auth/session`.
  - Compensating controls for unsigned exceptions include IP allowlist enforcement, auth-route per-IP throttling, credential lockout policy, and workstation-binding cookie validation on `/api/auth/session`.
  - Replay protection for signed routes uses nonce single-use records stored in MySQL with TTL, so replay history survives process restarts and multi-instance routing.
- Session-state restoration uses the server session endpoint and keeps the signing secret in memory only, while warm-lock resumes require PIN re-entry.
- Coach access scope is driven by explicit `coach_location_assignments` records rather than inferred from historical member/content data.
- Analytics filters are validated server-side before query execution, including malformed date-range rejection.
- Report exports are written to both a local backend reports directory and the configured shared-folder path.
- Scheduled reports persist their configured export format (`CSV`/`Excel`/`PDF`) and cron runs execute with that same format.
- Scheduled reports can target multiple subscribers, and inbox items expose direct download actions.
- Subscriber inbox access is available to authenticated recipients while schedule/template authoring remains administrator-only.
- Generated reports use the requesting administrator or schedule owner dashboard layout rather than a fixed administrator snapshot.
- The Reports surface loads template state directly, so generation and scheduling do not depend on visiting Dashboards first.
- Shared-folder delivery failures raise `storage_sync_error` alerts in the admin console.
- Admin metrics surface uptime, request latency, server error rate, and recent backup/report timings.
- Startup retries database initialization so `docker compose up` is resilient to MySQL readiness races.
- Nightly encrypted backups and dry-run restore workflows target the standby MySQL instance.
- The local key vault attempts to harden its file permissions on write; operators should still apply OS-level restrictions to the backend data directory.

## Verification checklist

- `docker compose up --build` starts all services without manual intervention.
- Backend readiness reports both API and database health.
- Admin, coach, and member sign-in flows work with role-appropriate navigation.
- `./run_tests.sh` passes end to end.
- Playwright verifies core admin workflow plus coach/member access boundaries.

content.text:
1. Verdict
- Partial Pass

2. Scope and Verification Boundary
- Reviewed: `README.md`, `docker-compose.yml`, backend auth/security/middleware/routes/services, frontend auth/API/core views, and the shipped backend/frontend/Playwright test suites.
- Executed: `backend\npm run typecheck` only. It completed successfully (`BACKEND_TYPECHECK_OK`).
- Not executed: `docker compose up --build`, `./run_tests.sh`, Playwright, or any Docker/MySQL runtime commands, per the review rule that forbids Docker execution.
- Docker-based verification was required by the project's canonical runtime and test path (`README.md:76-109`, `README.md:165-195`) but was not executed here.
- Remains unconfirmed: live Compose startup, real MySQL migration/startup behavior, scheduled background jobs, shared-folder report delivery, and end-to-end browser behavior against the actual runtime.

3. Top Findings
1. Severity: High
Conclusion: The frontend persists the per-session HMAC signing secret in `localStorage`.
Brief rationale: This turns a request-signing credential into long-lived browser-accessible state, increasing exposure to any injected script or compromised workstation profile.
Evidence: `frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:31-32` defines `sentinelfit.sessionSecret`; `frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:82-92` writes/reads it from `localStorage`; `frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:146-150`, `210`, `233`, `264` restore or persist it after bootstrap/login/PIN re-entry; `frontend/src/api/client.ts:76-88` uses that secret to produce signed API requests.
Impact: Weakens the authentication/session boundary for the most privileged operations because signed-request capability survives page reloads in browser storage rather than staying ephemeral.
Minimum actionable fix: Keep the signing secret in memory only, or move request-integrity proof to a server-managed `HttpOnly` mechanism that does not expose reusable signing material to frontend JavaScript.

2. Severity: High
Conclusion: The backend silently falls back to hardcoded weak MySQL credentials in non-production mode.
Brief rationale: The documented Compose runtime defaults to development mode, and on DB auth failure the backend retries with `sentinelfit` / `sentinelfit` instead of failing closed.
Evidence: `docker-compose.yml:43-57` defaults backend `NODE_ENV` to `development`; `backend/src/database.ts:70-97` retries with legacy credentials `sentinelfit` / `sentinelfit` and only disables that path in production.
Impact: Security posture can be downgraded without operator intent, which is especially risky for a system handling sessions, contact data, and biometric records.
Minimum actionable fix: Remove the automatic fallback, or gate it behind an explicit one-time migration flag that is off by default and loudly logged as insecure.

3. Severity: Medium
Conclusion: Local verification is not currently reproducible in this Windows workspace for the documented non-Docker commands.
Brief rationale: The repository claims local verification commands are useful, but the frontend/backend test commands and frontend typecheck did not resolve their local tool binaries here.
Evidence: `README.md:185-193` lists local verification commands; runtime results from this review: `backend\npm test` -> `'vitest' is not recognized...`, `frontend\npm test` -> `'vitest' is not recognized...`, `frontend\npm run typecheck` -> `'vue-tsc' is not recognized...`; only `backend\npm run typecheck` completed successfully.
Impact: Delivery confidence is reduced because I could not independently execute the shipped local unit/typecheck path without relying on Docker.
Minimum actionable fix: Make the local scripts resolve correctly on supported Windows shells, or document Docker as the only supported verification path and remove/qualify the local command claims.

4. Security Summary
- authentication: Partial Pass
Brief evidence or verification boundary: Password complexity, lockout, session expiry, logout revocation, workstation binding, and PIN re-entry are implemented in `backend/src/services/auth-service.ts:107-138`, `214-243`, `328-444`, `498-529`; however the frontend stores the signing secret in `localStorage` (`frontend/src/stores/auth.ts:82-92`).
- route authorization: Pass
Brief evidence or verification boundary: Route families are protected centrally in `backend/src/app.ts:75-109` with `requireSignedSession` and role gates; route tests cover admin/coach/member boundaries in `backend/tests/routes.test.ts:479-544` and `492-523`.
- object-level authorization: Pass
Brief evidence or verification boundary: Member access is checked in `backend/src/services/member-service.ts:149-169`; face access and dedup redaction in `backend/src/services/face-service.ts:338-395`; inbox download ownership in `backend/src/services/report-service.ts:569-589`.
- tenant / user isolation: Partial Pass
Brief evidence or verification boundary: User-level isolation is present for members, face records, and inbox downloads, but the system is effectively single-tenant and no tenant boundary exists to verify separately from prompt scope.

5. Test Sufficiency Summary
Test Overview

## 3. Top Findings

### Finding 1
- **Severity:** High
- **Conclusion:** Replay protection is process-local and can be bypassed after server restart within the timestamp validity window.
- **Brief rationale:** Nonce replay state is held in an in-memory `Map` in middleware; it is not persisted/shared, so restart clears replay history while active sessions may still exist.
- **Evidence:**
  - In-memory nonce store construction and usage: `backend/src/middleware.ts:20-43`, `backend/src/middleware.ts:157`
  - Signature window remains valid for ±5 minutes: `backend/src/middleware.ts:152-155`
- **Impact:** A captured signed request could potentially be replayed after process restart/failover if timestamp remains valid and session cookie is still active.
- **Minimum actionable fix:** Persist nonce records with TTL per session in a shared durable store (e.g., MySQL/Redis) and enforce single-use nonce across restarts/instances.

### Finding 2
- **Severity:** Medium
- **Conclusion:** Canonical delivery runnability is only partially verified in this audit.
- **Brief rationale:** The documented authoritative startup/test path is Docker-first, but Docker execution was not performed under the review constraints.

<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
